food_subgroup: Deep-fried fermented wheat batter spirals soaked in sugar syrup

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Jalebi. Nearest parent is a fried confectionery soaked in sugar syrup. A dedicated concept for jalebi-style fermented wheat spiral sweets is recommended for future FoodOn extension.
ontology.foodex2_description: Deep-fried wheat flour confection in sugar syrup, Indian (closest parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for jalebi)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for jalebi; A14AS used as closest defensible parent for deep-fried wheat confectionery in sugar syrup.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for jalebi or equivalent Indian fried sweet confectionery.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of jalebi is approximately 4 pieces (100 g), as commonly sold at Indian sweet shops and street food stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Jalebi — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F11100011; cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo Database food code F11100011 (Jalebi), cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables for secondary micronutrients. Both are approved primary sources for Indian dishes in the Singapore context.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database — Jalebi (deep-fried fermented wheat spiral in sugar syrup); GI referenced as very high due to predominantly sugar syrup composition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 82 referenced from University of Sydney GI Database for jalebi-type deep-fried wheat confectionery soaked in sugar syrup. The very high GI reflects the combination of refined wheat flour (maida) and rapid sucrose absorption from the sugar syrup. Glycemic load per 100 g serving calculated as (82 × 65.5) / 100 = 53.7, confirming extremely high glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (48 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Jalebi contains approximately 48 mg sodium per 100 g — well below the threshold of concern for hypertension — however, its very high sugar and caloric density may contribute to weight gain and metabolic syndrome, which are secondary risk factors for elevated blood pressure; moderate portion control is still advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic index (82) and very high sugar content (52 g per 100 g) producing a glycemic load of approximately 54 per 100 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Jalebi is among the most glycaemically dangerous foods for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes — its GI of 82 combined with 52 g of rapidly absorbed sucrose per 100 g produces a glycemic load of ~54, causing sharp postprandial glucose spikes; patients should avoid jalebi entirely or limit intake to 1 small piece (15–20 g) on rare festive occasions only, with blood glucose monitoring.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (12.8 g), high sugar (52 g), and deep-frying in oil contributing to atherogenic dietary pattern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Jalebi's combination of deep-frying (12.8 g fat per 100 g, including 1.8 g saturated fat) and extremely high sugar content (52 g per 100 g) promotes dyslipidaemia, insulin resistance, and visceral adiposity — all established cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat jalebi as an occasional indulgence only and limit serving size to one small piece.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very high sugar content (52 g per 100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory cytokines; deep-frying adds pro-inflammatory omega-6 fats
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should strictly limit jalebi — the combination of extremely high sugar and deep-frying represents one of the most pro-inflammatory dietary patterns; occasional consumption of one small piece on festive occasions only is the maximum advisable intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Jalebi is one of India's most iconic and vibrant street food sweets, popular across the Indian subcontinent, the Middle East, and among South Asian diaspora communities in Singapore and Southeast Asia. It is made by preparing a fermented batter of all-purpose flour (maida) and water, which is left to ferment for several hours or overnight to develop a characteristic mild tang. The batter is then piped through a cloth or squeeze bottle in tight spiral or pretzel shapes directly into hot oil and deep-fried until crispy and golden-orange. Immediately after frying, the hot spirals are immersed in warm sugar syrup flavoured with saffron and cardamom, which they absorb rapidly to become glisteningly orange, syrup-saturated, and intensely sweet. The defining eating experience is the contrast between the crispy fried exterior and the syrup-filled, juicy interior. Nutritionally, jalebi is very high in sugar (52 g per 100 g) and energy (380 kcal per 100 g), with moderate fat from deep-frying (12.8 g per 100 g) and low protein (3.2 g per 100 g). Its glycemic index is very high at approximately 82, producing a glycemic load of ~54 per 100 g — making it one of the most glycaemically impactful foods available. Jalebi is clinically contraindicated for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes and should be consumed only occasionally by those with cardiovascular disease or inflammatory conditions. In Singapore, jalebi is sold at Indian sweet shops and is a popular festive sweet during Deepavali celebrations.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo Database (food code F11100011, exact match for Jalebi) cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables — both are approved primary sources. GI referenced from University of Sydney GI Database. All core macros and secondary nutrients are complete and internally consistent.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for jalebi not separately reported in HPB Foodo Database (F11100011) or ICMR-NIN tables as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for jalebi not reported in HPB Foodo Database or ICMR-NIN as of April 2026. trans_fat_g reported as 0.0 — confirmed negligible per HPB source. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, and vitamin_d_mcg reported as 0.0 — confirmed negligible per HPB source; jalebi contains no significant vitamin C, vitamin A, or vitamin D by composition. Duplicate portion entries in input (three identical '1 serving (50g)' entries) consolidated to one representative portion; canonical serving retained as 4 pieces / 100 g per verified HPB source. Portions input had multiple is_default:true entries — resolved to single default of 100 g. FoodEx2 code A14AS used as closest parent; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for jalebi. GI of 82 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for jalebi-type deep-fried wheat confectionery in sugar syrup; glycemic load calculated as (82 × 65.5) / 100 = 53.7 per 100 g serving.
